coredata.dc:title: A multifunctional sensor for selective and sensitive detection of vitamin B12 and tartrazine by Förster resonance energy transfer
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yingping
Jia, Yong
Zeng, Qi
Jiang, Xiaohui
Cheng, Zhengjun
coredata.dc:description: We used thiamine nitrate (TN) as single material to fabricate nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs) with a quantum yield of 10.4% through one-pot hydrothermal method, and its properties were characterized by TEM, XPS, FTIR, fluorescence (FL) and UV–vis spectrophotometer, respectively. The fluorescence of N,S-CQDs was effectively quenched in the presence of vitamin B12 (VB12)/tartrazine due to Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). Moreover, the rate (K T) and efficiency (E%) of energy transfer from N,S-CQDs (as a donor) to VB12/tartrazine (as an acceptor) enhanced with increasing the concentrations of acceptor, and the K T and E% were also varied with the change of excitation wavelengths (from 338 to 408 nm). Based on this principle, a multifunctional fluorescence probe was designed for selective and sensitive detection of VB12/tartrazine with a detection limit (3σ/slope) of 15.6/18.0 nmol/L. Meanwhile, the proposed method was successfully employed to detect VB12/tartrazine in milk and several beverages with a recovery range of 97.5–104.2%/91.0–110.6%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N,S-CQDs
FRET
Multifunctional sensor
Vitamin B12
Tartrazine

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General Procedure of Spectrofluorimetric Detection for VB12/Tartrazine
Characterization and Optical Properties of N,S-CQDs
Fluorescence Response of N,S-CQDs to VB12/Tartrazine

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of the N,S-CQDs (insert: the size distribution of N,S-CQDs); B. XPS survey spectrum of N,S-CQDs.

A. FL excitation (λem = 416 nm) and emission (λex = 338 nm) spectra of N,S-CQDs and UV–vis absorption spectra of TN (green line) and N,S-CQDs (red line) (inset: the photos of N,S-CQDs under daylight (left bottle) and UV-light of 365 nm (right bottle); B. FL spectra of N,S-CQDs with different excitation wavelengths (from 288 to 408 nm); C. The normalized FL spectra of N,S-CQDs at λex = 288–408 nm (in 10 nm increment); D. The quantum yield of N,S-CQDs at λex = 338–408 nm (in 10 nm increment).

A. The spectral overlap of UV–vis absorption spectrum of VB12 and FL spectrum of N,S-CQDs; B. The spectral overlap of UV–vis absorption spectrum of tartrazine and FL spectrum of N,S-CQDs; C. The FL quenching of N,S-CQDs by VB12 (C VB12 = 0–114.61 μmol/L, insert: the relationship between (F 0 − F) / F and C VB12); D. The FL quenching of N,S-CQDs by tartrazine (C tartrazine = 0–70.26 μmol/L, insert: the relationship between (F 0 − F) / F and C tartrazine).

The changes of energy transfer efficiency (E%) and donor-acceptor distance (r) (A or B), and rate of energy transfer (K T) (C or D) upon increasing the concentrations of VB12 or tartrazine (0.33–57.93 or 0.033–36.56 μmol/L).

The variations of energy transfer efficiency (E%, A and D), donor-acceptor distance (r, B and E) and rate of energy transfer (K T, C and F) of the N,S-CQDs-VB12 and N,S-CQDs-tartrazine systems, respectively (C N,S-CQDs = 0.292 mg/mL, C VB12 = 0.33–57.93 μmol/L, C tartrazine = 0.033–36.56 μmol/L, λex = 338–408 nm).

A. Time resolved fluorescence decay curves of prepared N,S-CQDs (0.292 mg/mL) in the absence and presence of VB12 (66.67 μmol/L) or tartrazine (33.33 μmol/L) at λex = 338 nm and λem = 416 nm; B. UV–vis absorption spectra of N,S-CQDs (0.0292 mg/mL), VB12 (7.0 μmol/L), experimental N,S-CQDs-VB12 (the experimental curve denotes the absorption spectrum of N,S-CQDs in the presence of VB12), theoretical N,S-CQDs-VB12 (the theoretical curve denotes the physical addition of the spectra of N,S-CQDs and VB12).

A. The relationship between the FL quenched efficiency and the concentration of VB12; B. The selectivity of N,S-CQDs to different materials with the concentration of 100 μmol/L; C. The FL intensity ratios of N,S-CQDs in the presence of VB12 (100 μmol/L, blank) with different coexistent materials (their concentrations are kept at 500 μmol/L).
Schematic illustration of synthesis of N,S-CQDs by TN and sensing vitamin B12 or tartrazine.
The intra-day and inter-day precision of the probe for detecting VB12/tartrazine (n = 3).
The determination of VB12 in milk and two kinds of vitamin drinks (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A multifunctional sensor for selective and sensitive detection of vitamin B12 and tartrazine by Förster resonance energy transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, China West Normal University, Nanchong 637002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Applied Chemistry, China West Normal University, Nanchong 637002, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, China West Normal University, Nanchong 637002, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We used thiamine nitrate (TN) as single material to fabricate nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs) with a quantum yield of 10.4% through one-pot hydrothermal method, and its properties were characterized by TEM, XPS, FTIR, fluorescence (FL) and UV–vis spectrophotometer, respectively. The fluorescence of N,S-CQDs was effectively quenched in the presence of vitamin B12 (VB12)/tartrazine due to Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). Moreover, the rate (K T) and efficiency (E%) of energy transfer from N,S-CQDs (as a donor) to VB12/tartrazine (as an acceptor) enhanced with increasing the concentrations of acceptor, and the K T and E% were also varied with the change of excitation wavelengths (from 338 to 408 nm). Based on this principle, a multifunctional fluorescence probe was designed for selective and sensitive detection of VB12/tartrazine with a detection limit (3σ/slope) of 15.6/18.0 nmol/L. Meanwhile, the proposed method was successfully employed to detect VB12/tartrazine in milk and several beverages with a recovery range of 97.5–104.2%/91.0–110.6%.

N,S-CQDs as a multifunctional sensor has remarkable selectivity and high sensitivity for monitoring VB12/tartrazine, and the FL quenching of N,S-CQDs by VB12/tartrazine was further analyzed by FRET theory. The r values decrease with increasing the concentration of acceptor (VB12/tartrazine) (A/B), and corresponding E% and K T values (C/D) show an opposite change tendency.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Vitamin B12 (VB12) has a Co2+ coordinated by four nitrogen atoms of a corrin ring and a fifth nitrogen atom from an imidazole group [1], which plays a vital role in the lipid, carbohydrate metabolism, nerve cell maintenance, DNA synthesis and repair, the metabolism of enzyme, especially the bone marrow tissue responsible for red blood cell formation and cardiovascular system [2–5]. But VB12 cannot be synthesized by the body and can be prepared by microbial and higher flora. Besides, it can also be obtained from animal-derived foods (such as meat, dairy products, egg, fish, shellfish and poultry), or plant-derived foods (like various Chlorella supplements) [6,7]. The acceptable daily intake (ADI) of VB12 for human is 1‐2 μg [8]. The lack of VB12 may lead to some diseases including pernicious anemia, memory loss and nerve degeneration [9]. Meanwhile, excessive VB12 can also cause liver disease, neurotoxicity, kidney failure, or myeloproliferative disorders [10]. Tartrazine as a food colorant is usually added into foods to make them become more attractive and appealing [11,12], and its dosage should be strictly controlled by laws and regulations. However, tartrazine has a mutagenic and carcinogenic possibility due to the presence of azo group (NN) [13], particularly when it is excessively consumed. Thus, it is of great significance to monitor the content of VB12/tartrazine in the foods. In the past decades, some common methods (like high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [14], enzyme-linked immune-sorbent assay (ELISA) [15], chemiluminescence [16], liquid chromatography/electrospray ionization-tandem mass spectrometry (LC/ESI-MS/MS) [17], liquid chromatography-flame atomic absorption spectrometric (LC-AAS) [2], capillary electrophoresis [18] and electrochemical method [19–21], etc.) are widely employed to quantify VB12/tartrazine. Nevertheless, these techniques require complicated procedures, costly instruments, relatively expensive reagents or environmentally unfriendly, which will limit their practical applications. Therefore, it is increasingly urgent to explore novel probes for solving the above difficult problems.

Recently, fluorescence sensors especially carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted great interests in bio-imaging [22], bio-sensing [23], chemical sensing [24], drug delivery [25], photo-catalysis [26], electro-catalysis [27], energy conversion and storage [28] owing to their good biocompatibility, excellent photo-stability, negligible cytotoxicity, low cost, chemical inertness, water solubility, and so on [29–31]. However, challenges remain for CQDs such as poor selectivity, low sensitivity and some difficulty in the synthesis of a large amount of CQDs powders with uniform morphology [32]. To surmount the drawbacks of bare CQDs, heteroatoms (such as nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus, boron, fluorine, etc.) doping into the carbon skeleton is employed which can adjust its surface and local chemical properties [33]. As a result, doped CQDs can not only enhance fluorescence properties but also the selectivity of target sensing via available functional groups from the surface of CQDs [34–36], especially co-doping with two heteroatoms provides more improved properties than single heteroatom doping does [37]. In recent years, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CQDs (N,S-CQDs) has been frequently studied because the atomic radius of nitrogen and carbon is close, while the electronegativity of sulfur is similar to that of carbon [38], and the CQDs has been used in different fields. For example, N,S-CQDs was first synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal treatment using citric acid (as a carbon precursor) and l-cysteine (as a nitrogen and sulfur source). The prepared N,S-CQDs shows a excitation-independent emission behavior because the original surface states in the N,S-CQDs nearly are neglected by potentially eliminating the O-states and enhancing the N-state after the introduced sulfur atom, and has great application potential in bio-imaging and bio-sensing [34]. In the same year, Sun et al. [39] exploited a large-scale synthesis of S-N-C-dots using carbonization and etching of hair fiber with sulfuric acid, and the S-N-C-dots exhibited smaller size, higher content of S, and longer emission wavelength when higher reaction temperature was obtained. Furthermore, it was used as an eco-friendly material in bio-labeling. Following, a sensitive, low-cost and green fluorescence probe (N,S-CQDs) was synthesized by Zhao's team, which was employed to monitor free chlorine with a wide linear range of 0.01–100 μmol/L and a low detection limit of 5 nmol/L [40]. Rogach's group [41] has evaluated fully the absorption, photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra of N-doped and N,S-co-doped CQDs with three emission bands (the core, edge and surface) in two different solvents (protic water (H2O) and aprotic dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO)), respectively. Later, a similar route and using different nitrogen and sulfur materials, the S,N-co-doped CQDs developed by Miao et al. showed high photoluminescence quantum yield (29% and 22% in ethanol and water) of red emission which was beneficial to application in the theranostic field. Then a theranostic agent (CD-Dox, i.e. doxorubicin was loaded onto carbon quantum dots) was injected into the tumor site, and both size and weight of the tumor decreased obviously after 7 times injection. Meanwhile, the concentration of theranostic agent could be also traced by FL intensity of the S,N-CDs/Dox [42]. Recently, a novel, green and dual turn-off/on probe (N,S-CQDs, citric acid, urea and thiourea as starting materials) was synthesized by the microwave-assisted method, and used to detect Hg2+ (turn off) and I− (turn on) in tap, river, mineral waters and fish samples, respectively [43]. However, these N,S-CQDs reported previously were synthesized using two or more ingredients which could increase the difficulty of controlling synthesis process and reduce the yield of N,S-CQDs owing to producing the byproducts. While the prepared N,S-CQDs by single starting material containing N and S can simplify the synthesis process and simultaneously decrease the byproducts [44].

In addition, a few researchers have also used CQDs or heteroatom doped CQDs (like T-CDs [3], N-CQDs [45] and N,P-CQDs [6] for VB12, while Citrus limon CQDs [29] and Aloe C-dots [31] for tartrazine) for the determination of VB12 or tartrazine with a satisfactory result. Up to now, the detection of VB12 or tartrazine by a novel sensor based on FRET theory has not been reported. While the FRET theory was employed to explore the interaction between MoS2 quantum dots (MoS2 QDs) and Rhodamine 6G (R6G), and the study discovered that the energy was transferred from MoS2 QDs (donor) to R6G (acceptor), and the energy transfer efficiency (E%) and resonant energy transfer rate (K T) enhanced with increasing the excitation wavelengths of MoS2 QDs [46]. Later, their group [47] also used the FRET mechanism for exploring the FL quenching of MoS2 QDs by PANI via steady state and time resolved fluorescence spectroscopy, and the changes of E% and K T were proportional to the concentrations of PANI. Meanwhile, the quenched fluorescence of MoS2-PANI system was gradually recovered with the addition of BSA at nmol level. On this basis, we have developed a multi-function sensor (N,S-CQDs) for the determination of VB12/tartrazine (Scheme 1 ), and the action mechanism of two systems is further discussed through FRET theory. Meanwhile, the synthesis conditions (like the dosage of TN, time and temperature) of N,S-CQDs were optimized. The prepared N,S-CQDs exhibits a bright blue fluorescence, excellent water solubility, salt tolerance and pH stability. The effects of detection conditions including temperature, time and pH were also discussed. Moreover, the sensor was utilized to analyze VB12/tartrazine in milk and beverages, respectively.

Thiamine nitrate (TN, 98.0%) and vitamin B12 (VB12, AR) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tartrazine (95.0%) was bought from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The Britton-Robison (B-R) buffer with different pH values was prepared by adding diverse amounts of 0.2 mol/L of NaOH to 0.04 mol/L of the mixture of acetic acid, boric acid and phosphoric acid. Other reagents (see Supporting information) are analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water is used throughout the experiment.

The microstructure and size of N,S-CQDs were analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with a JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan) at 200 kV. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured on a Nicolet-6700 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with a pressed KBr pellet and the range of 400–4000 cm−1. The surface functional properties of N,S-CQDs were investigated by ESCALAB250Xi PHI Quantera II X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Fluorescence lifetimes were implemented using a FLS980 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). All fluorescence and absorption spectra were recorded on a Cary eclipse fluorescence spectrofluorimeter (VARIAN, USA) and a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), respectively.

The N,S-CQDs was prepared using previously reported method with minor modifications [48]. Briefly, 0.2 g of TN was added into 20 mL of ultrapure water and stirred for 15 min at 45 °C. Then the clear solution was transferred into a 50 mL of poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave, and heated at 180 °C for 10 h and then cooled down to room temperature. The brown liquid obtained was centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm polypropylene filter membrane to remove large particles. The prepared N,S-CQDs was purified by dialysis against ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (MWCO of 100 Da) for 48 h. Finally, the resulting solution containing N,S-CQDs was dried in a vacuum drying oven at 60 °C for 72 h and the obtained solid was stored for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General Procedure of Spectrofluorimetric Detection for VB12/Tartrazine
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical assay, a suitable volume of VB12/tartrazine solution and 100 μL of N,S-CQDs (8.75 mg/mL) were diluted to 3.0 mL through the Britton-Robinson (B-R) buffer of pH 7.0. After the mixed solutions were incubated for 5 min, their FL spectra were recorded from 340 to 600 nm (λex = 338 nm) at room temperature. Both excitation and emission slit widths were set at 5 nm. The selectivity and interference of the proposed sensor for VB12/tartrazine detection were also evaluated with the addition of anyone of coexistent materials in a similar way. All the experiments were implemented in triplicate.

Quantum yield of N,S-CQDs is measured through quinine sulfate as a reference standard in 0.1 mol/L of H2SO4 (Φ s = 54%) and its value is calculated according to the following formula [49]:
(1)
Φ = Φ s
I / A
A s
/
I s
η /
η s
2
where Φ is quantum yield, I and A represent the emission intensity and absorbance value, respectively; the subscript ‘s’ refers to the reference standard with a known quantum yield; η is the refractive index of the solvent.

Föster resonance energy transfer (FRET) will happen under these conditions: (i) the donor can produce fluorescence light, (ii) the donor's fluorescence emission spectrum overlaps the absorbance spectrum of the acceptor, and (iii) the distance between donor and acceptor is small (1–10 nm). The efficiency of energy transfer (E%) is calculated according to Eq. (2) [50]:
(2)
E = 1 – F /
F 0
=
R 0 6
/
R 0 6
+
r 6

In Eq. (2), r is the distance between donor and acceptor; R 0 represents the critical distance when the transfer efficiency equals to 50%, and its value is estimated using the equation:
(3)
R 0 6
= 8.8 ×
10
− 25
k 2
n
− 4
ΦJ
where k 2 is the orientation factor related to the geometry of donor-acceptor dipole and is assumed to be 2/3; n is the refractive index of medium; Φ is the fluorescence quantum yield of the donor in the absence of acceptor; and J is the spectral overlap integral between the fluorescence emission spectrum of donor and the absorption spectrum of acceptor. The value of J is calculated by the following equation:
(4)
J =
∫ 0 ∞
F
λ
ε
λ
λ 4
dλ
∫ 0 ∞
F
λ
dλ
where F(λ) and ε(λ) denote the FL intensity of donor and the molar absorption coefficient of acceptor at wavelength λ, respectively.

The resonance energy transfer rate (K T) is given by Eq. (5) [46]:
(5)
K T
=
1
τ D
R 0
r
6
where τ D is the lifetime of the donor in the absence of the acceptor.

VB12 may be added into milk or vitamin drinks as one of nutrient contents and tartrazine is added into energy drinks or soda drink as a colorant, therefore, they are selected as test samples. Milk and drinks were bought from a local supermarket in Nanchong, China. Milk was pretreated by the method of a literature with some modifications [51]. 50 mL of milk was heated at 75 °C and then 0.5 mol/L of HCl was added slowly to the milk until pH equaled to 5.0 under stirring in a water bath. After the reaction for 30 min, the resulting suspension was cooled to room temperature. Treated milk and 10 mL of other sample (vitamin drinks, energy drinks or soda drink) were centrifuged for 15 min at 9000 rpm respectively, and the corresponding supernatant was filtered using a 0.22 μm polypropylene filter membrane. The mixed solution containing 1.5 mL of processed milk or vitamin drinks with a certain amount of VB12 (0, 0.015, 0.030 or 0.045 μmol) and 100 μL of N,S-CQDs (8.75 mg/mL) was diluted to 3.0 mL with the B-R buffer (pH = 7.0) for testing. Meanwhile, 0.6 mL of processed energy drinks or soda drink containing a certain amount of tartrazine (0, 0.006, 0.015 or 0.030 μmol) was mixed with 100 μL of N,S-CQDs (8.75 mg/mL) solution and diluted to 3.0 mL using the B-R buffer of pH 7.0. The above mixed solutions obtained were taken out for analysis according to the determination procedure, respectively.

In order to achieve the N,S-CQDs with wonderful photoluminescence performance, the synthesis conditions were optimized. Firstly, the study of TN dosage (0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4 and 0.6 g) implied that 0.2 g of TN (Fig. S1A) mixed in 20.0 mL of ultrapure water had the maximal FL intensity in the hydrothermal synthesis process. Secondly, the FL intensity of N,S-CQDs synthesized at 10 h was strongest in the reaction time range of 4–12 h (Fig. S1B). Finally, when the reaction temperature varied from 140 to 180 °C, the FL intensities of N,S-CQDs gradually increased, and became almost invariable after 180 °C (Fig. S1C). In a word, the optimized preparation conditions of N,S-CQDs were summarized as follows: 0.2 g of TN was dissolved in 20.0 mL of ultrapure water, then sealed into a 50.0 mL of poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 10 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and Optical Properties of N,S-CQDs

ce:section.ce:para: The prepared N,S-CQDs was characterized by various techniques. Its size and morphology were obtained through transmission electron microcopy (TEM) and listed in Fig. 1A. The synthesized N,S-CQDs was well mono-dispersed without obvious aggregation. In the selected region, the sizes of N,S-CQDs were mainly distributed in a narrow range of 1.0–2.5 nm, and the average size was estimated to be 1.5 nm (Fig. 1A, insert) which was similar to previously reported carbon quantum dots [52].

To investigate the surface functional groups and composition of N,S-CQDs, its FTIR spectra were studied. As shown in Fig. S2, a broad peak at 3142 cm−1 in the FTIR of N,S-CQDs resulted from the stretching vibration of OH/NH [53]. Peaks at 1664, 1606, 1542 and 1504 cm−1 correlated with the CC, CO and CN stretching vibration [54–56], respectively. The bending vibration of NH or the starching vibration of CN appeared at 1384 cm−1. In addition, the absorption bands in 1199, 1117 and 1068 cm−1 indicated that the CS and CO bond existed [51,57]. The results of FTIR imply that the surfaces of N,S-CQDs have rich amino, carbonyl and hydroxyl groups. Meanwhile, these functional groups can enhance the stability and hydrophilicity of N,S-CQDs in a water-based system.

The surface components and functional state of N,S-CQDs were further identified by X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS). The XPS survey spectrum of N,S-CQDs displayed that the peaks of S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s were observed at 164.1, 284.9, 399.5 and 532.1 eV (Fig. 1B), respectively. The high resolution C1s spectrum of N,S-CQDs (Fig. S3A) exhibited five main peaks at CC/CC (284.9 eV), CS (285.7 eV), CN (286.2 eV), CO (286.7 eV) and CN/CO (288.5 eV) [58], which could be attributed to sp2 carbon (CC/CC), sp3 carbon (CS/CN/CO) and CN/CO, respectively [34,59]. The result was in accordance well with its FTIR analysis. Three peaks in N1s high-resolution spectrum indicated the binding energy at 401.3 eV for sp3 hybrid nitrogen (NH), 400.5 eV for pyrrolic nitrogen (CN) and 399.5 eV for pyridinic nitrogen (CNC/CN) bonds (Fig. S3B) [57], which affirmed that N atoms were presented in the surface of N,S-CQDs. From the S2p high-resolution spectrum, two fitted peaks at 164.1 and 164.6 eV could be assigned to S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 (Fig. S3C) [57]. In conclusion, the analysis of FTIR and XPS clearly verified that nitrogen and sulfur functional groups existed on the surface of N,S-CQDs.

Fluorescence behavior under different excitation wavelengths and UV–vis absorption spectrum of N,S-CQDs were recorded to discuss its optical properties. The UV–vis spectrum of N,S-CQDs (Fig. 2A) exhibited a strong absorption peak at 248 nm, which should be attributed to π-π* transition of CC/CN bond from aromatic sp2 domains [60]. Additionally, a weak absorption peak at 339 nm could be assigned to n-π* transition of CO bond. The color of prepared N,S-CQDs solution was light brown (insert of Fig. 2A, left bottle) under daylight and displayed a bright blue fluorescence under UV illumination of 365 nm (insert of Fig. 2A, right bottle), suggesting that the luminescent carbon nanoparticles were formed. The maximum emission wavelength of N,S-CQDs appears at 416 nm when the excitation wavelength is kept at 338 nm (Fig. 2B), and its emission peak has a significant red-shifted (from 401 to 466 nm) at different excitation wavelengths (in the range of 288–408 nm) (Fig. 2C). The result hints an excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior, which may be ascribed to the different size particles and the distribution of emissive trap sites on the surface of N,S-CQDs [61,62]. In addition, the quantum yield of N,S-CQDs was calculated to be 10.4% at λex = 338 nm. Furthermore, its quantum yield values reduce with the increase of excitation wavelengths from 338 to 408 nm (Fig. 2D), which reconfirms that the N,S-CQDs possesses an excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior.

In addition, the stability of N,S-CQDs has been investigated by inquiring the effects of temperature (10–40 °C), pH (3−11), salt concentration (0–500 mmol/L), preservation time under natural light (1–30 day) and UV light of 365 nm (0–60 min) (Fig. S4A–E). The results of study discover that the prepared N,S-CQDs shows a relatively constant fluorescence emission at broad ranges of temperature, pH, ionic strength and preservation time, and is highly resistant to photo-bleaching, which makes that the probe has a great potential for sensing applications in normal environment.

The absorption spectrum of VB12/tartrazine shows obvious characteristic absorption with the range of 330–600/300–500 nm (Fig. 3A/B), which is attributed to the HOMO-LUMO transitions [63]. The observed intense emission of N,S-CQDs at 416 nm is related to the molecular states and different surface structures [64]. It is interesting to notice that the FL emission spectrum of N,S-CQDs is partly overlapped with the absorption spectrum of VB12/tartrazine. This phenomenon suggests that it is possible to happen a FRET between N,S-CQDs and VB12/tartrazine. That is to say, the N,S-CQDs acts as a donor and VB12/tartrazine serves as an acceptor [46]. To examine FRET process experimentally, we recorded steady state fluorescence data of N,S-CQDs (as a donor, 0.292 mg/mL) with the addition of VB12/tartrazine (as an acceptor) (Fig. 3C/D), and corresponding FL intensities around 416 nm gradually reduced with the increase in the concentrations of VB12/tartrazine (the range of 0–114.61/0–70.26 μmol/L). This was due to the N,S-CQDs getting excited for the given excitation wavelength (λex = 338 nm) and emitting its broad fluorescence around 350–560 nm with a maximum peak at 416 nm. VB12/tartrazine can accept the energy donated by N,S-CQDs, which clearly indicates that the prepared N,S-CQDs and VB12/tartrazine serve as a donor and an acceptor, respectively. N,S-CQDs is donating its energy (which is accepted by VB12/tartrazine), so the FL of N,S-CQDs is gradually quenched, meanwhile without any shift is observed at its maximum emission wavelength with increasing the concentration of acceptor (VB12/tartrazine), which confirms that the energy is transferred from the N,S-CQDs to VB12/tartrazine. The phenomenon further testifies that a FRET is happened between the N,S-CQDs and VB12/tartrazine and it is well consistent with spectral overlap between them. However, the donor-acceptor distance (r) can affect the energy transfer efficiency (E%) between them and it will alter with the acceptor concentration. The values of E% and r are calculated using Eqs. (2)–(4), respectively. As observed in Fig. 4A/B, the values of r decrease with increasing the concentration of acceptor (VB12/tartrazine), and corresponding values of E% show the opposite change. Meanwhile, we have found that E% increases when r decreases, i.e. shorter the r, higher the E%. K T (the rate of energy transfer) is calculated according to Eq. (5). The changes of K T with enhancing the concentration of VB12/tartrazine are listed in Fig. 4C/D. The values of K T are observed to be increased when the concentrations of acceptor are enhanced.

Since the prepared N,S-CQDs has an excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior, there will be considerable effect on energy transfer process between the donor and the acceptor at different excitation wavelengths [46]. Following, we recorded steady state fluorescence of N,S-CQDs-VB12/tartrazine pair at different excitation wavelengths (from 338 to 408 nm, an interval of 10 nm) with increasing the concentration of VB12/tartrazine, and the corresponding values of E%, r and K T were calculated by Eqs. (2)–(5). The values calculated were shown in Fig. 5 . At the other single excitation wavelength (348, 358, 368, 378, 388, 398 or 408 nm), E% and K T of N,S-CQDs-VB12/tartrazine pair increased and their values of r decreased with the increase of VB12/tartrazine concentration (Fig. 5A–F), which was well consistent with the variation tendency at the excitation wavelength of 338 nm. Furthermore, all the E%, r and K T values altered when the excitation wavelengths shifted from 338 to 408 nm. As shown in Fig. 5A–C, the E% and K T are larger and r is smaller at λex = 358 nm than those under other excitation wavelengths with the same concentration of VB12, indicating that the energy transfer level between N,S-CQDs (donor) and VB12 (acceptor) is the highest under the excitation wavelength of 358 nm. In the same way, compared to other excitation wavelengths, the energy transfer from N,S-CQDs to tartrazine is more efficient at λex = 368 nm (Fig. 5D–F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence Response of N,S-CQDs to VB12/Tartrazine
ce:section.ce:para: As presented in Fig. 3C/D, the FL intensities of N,S-CQDs at 416 nm clearly reduced with the addition of VB12/tartrazine, which hinted that N,S-CQDs did respond to VB12/tartrazine. To further investigate the FL quenching mechanism of N,S-CQDs in the presence of VB12/tartrazine, fluorescence lifetime, UV–vis absorption and FL spectra experiments were carried out. As shown in Fig. 6A, compared with the lifetime of only N,S-CQDs (τ 1 = 2.25 ns (91.92%), τ 2 = 6.00 ns (8.08%), and the average lifetime is calculated to be 2.96 ns), its lifetime (τ 1 = 5.66/10.60 ns (10.21%/2.32%), τ2 = 2.33/2.91 ns (89.79%/97.68%), and the average lifetime is calculated to be 3.05/3.53 ns) changes slightly in the presence of VB12/tartrazine, which reveals that the FL quenching of N,S-CQDs by VB12/tartrazine belongs to a static quenching process. Meanwhile, the multi-exponential property of the lifetime suggests that the N,S-CQDs has been present in different emissive sites [65]. As shown in Figs. 6B/S5, the experimental curve (the absorption spectrum of N,S-CQDs-VB12/tartrazine system) was inconsistent with the theoretical curve (the physical addition of the spectra of N,S-CQDs and VB12/tartrazine solution), hinting that the stable N,S-CQDs-VB12/tartrazine complex was formed. The phenomenon also revealed that a static quenching occurred between the N,S-CQDs and VB12/tartrazine [64]. Besides, the fluorescence data of two systems at 416 nm (Fig. 3C/D) were also analyzed by the Stern-Volmer equation (F 0/F = 1 + K sv[Q] = 1 + K q τ 0[Q], where F 0 and F represent the FL intensities in the absence and presence of VB12/tartrazine, respectively; K SV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant; K q is the bimolecular quenching rate constant; τ 0 is the lifetime of N,S-CQDs; and [Q] denotes the concentration of VB12/tartrazine). While the K SV value of N,S-CQDs-VB12/tartrazine system was yielded to be 1.98 × 104/4.79 × 104 L mol−1 by the slope of linear Stern-Volmer plot (Figs. 7A/S6A), and its K q value (K q = K SV/τ 0 = 6.69 × 1014/1.62 × 1013 L mol−1 s−1, τ 0 = 2.96 ns) was higher than maximum K q value for a diffusion controlled quenching process (about 1010 L mol−1 s−1) [66], which further hinted that the quenching was mainly arisen from the static quenching by the formation of N,S-CQDs-VB12/tartrazine complex.

For a static quenching interaction, there is independent binding site during the quenching process. The binding constant (K) and the number of binding site (n) can be calculated by the Benesi-Hildebrand equation [67]:
(6)
1
F 0
− F
=
1
K
F 0
−
F 1
Q
n
+
1
F 0
−
F 1

F 1 notes the emission intensity of N,S-CQDs (λem = 416 nm) at a concentration of complete interaction. When n equals to 1, 1 / [F 0 − F] vs. 1 / [Q] can be fitted with a good linearity (R 2 = 0.9996/0.9951) for the N,S-CQDs-VB12/tartrazine system (Fig. S7A/B), which implies that the stoichiometric ratio between N,S-CQDs and VB12/tartrazine is kept at 1:1. The corresponding binding constant equals to 6.48 × 104/2.31 × 104 L mol−1 by the slope of the linear fitting equation, suggesting that the binding affinity of N,S-CQDs with VB12/tartrazine is approximate to that of N-GQD with Al3+ [67].

To obtain the best performance for the VB12/tartrazine detection, the influences of reaction time, temperature and pH were optimized (Figs. S8/S9). As shown in Figs. S8/S9, the optimal reaction time, temperature and pH for the N,S-CQDs-VB12/tartrazine system were settled at 5 min, room temperature and neutral environment, respectively.

Fig. 3C/D demonstrated the FL spectra of N,S-CQDs after adding different concentrations of VB12/tartrazine under the optimized conditions, and its FL intensities decreased gradually with increasing the concentration of VB12/tartrazine (0–114.61/0–70.26 μmol/L). There was two good linear relationships between (F 0 − F) / F and C VB12/C tartrazine (the concentration of VB12/tartrazine) in the ranges of 0.33–28.30/0.06–11.16 μmol/L (R 2 = 0.9994/0.9993) and 28.30–74.85/11.16–36.56 μmol/L (R 2 = 0.9972/0.9946) (Figs. 7A/S6A) with a detection limit of 15.6/18.0 nmol/L (based on the equation 3σ / slope, σ is the standard deviation). For the N,S-CQDs-VB12 system, the two linear fitting equations are (F 0 − F) / F = 0.0198 × C VB12 + 0.0227 and (F 0 − F) / F = 0.0370 × C VB12 − 0.4726; while for the N,S-CQDs-tartrazine system, they are described as (F 0 − F) / F = 0.0479 × C tartrazine + 0.0245 and (F 0 − F) / F = 0.0889 × C tartrazine − 0.4589. In addition, we have compared the linear range and detection limit of VB12/tartrazine based on the previously reported methods (Tables S1/S2). The detection limit of VB12 (or tartrazine) by the N,S-CQDs probe is lower than those of VB12 (or tartrazine) by other methods except electrochemical techniques with self-catalytic carbon paste electrode and Au/PPy/FMNPs@TD-modified electrode (or electrochemical techniques with molecularly imprinted copolymer modified carbon electrode and graphene oxide gold nanoparticles modified electrode, and ELISA) respectively, but the linear range of VB12 (or tartrazine) by the proposed probe is wider than those of two kinds of methods (or three kinds of methods). Therefore, the FL sensor is worth considering for the determination of VB12/tartrazine because it possesses a wider linear range and a lower detection limit.

To further investigate the selectivity of N,S-CQDs for VB12/tartrazine, the effects of some representative metal ions (Al3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Ag+, Ba2+, K+, Cr3+, Mg2+ and Co2+), amino acids (GSH, L-Cys, L-Lys, L-Try, L-Ala, Gly, L-Val, L-Asp, L-Glu. L-Met, L-Ser, L-His, L-AA, L-Leu, L-Tyr and L-Pro), vitamins (VH, AA, TN and VB6), food colorants (PB, AR 1, Orange ΙΙ and AG 9) for tartrazine and food additives (SHMP, PS, DG, NC, Inositol, Sucrose, AK, BA, Xylitol, Taurine, CA and Starch) on the FL quenching of N,S-CQDs were evaluated at the same conditions, respectively (Figs. 7B/S6B). The FL quenching ratios (F 0/F) of all substances except for VB12/tartrazine were close to 1, and VB12/tartrazine had a high quenching ratio (~5.96/5.19). Moreover, some of the above substances have been selected to study interference experiments of detecting VB12/tartrazine by N,S-CQDs because they may act as potential interfering substances in real samples. Then we calculated the FL intensity ratios of N,S-CQDs-VB12/tartrazine system (C VB12 = 100 μmol/L and C tartrazine = 50 μmol/L) in the presence of anyone of interfering substances (their concentrations were set at 500/250 μmol/L for VB12/tartrazine), respectively (Figs. 7C/S6C). The interfering substances had negligible influence on the detection of VB12/tartrazine by the N,S-CQDs probe. All the results reveal that the probe presents excellent selectivity and sensitivity for monitoring VB12/tartrazine.

To evaluate the practicability and reliability of developed sensor in the real samples, primarily, the reliability and precision of the method were discussed by analyzing the results of VB12/tartrazine detection in the intra-day and inter-day. As shown in Table 1 , their relative standard deviations (RSD) are less than 1.72%, indicating that the probe proposed is reliable for monitoring VB12/tartrazine. Next, we have selected milk and two kinds of vitamin drinks for the determination of VB12, and two kinds of energy dinks and soda drink for the tartrazine determination based on standard addition experiments, respectively. According to the procedures described in the Section 2.6, corresponding results were calculated and listed in Table 2 . No VB12 was detected in milk or vitamin drink 2. The recovery and RSD of the probe were in the range of 97.5%–104.2% and lower than 2.09%. Meanwhile, tartrazine in the real samples was also measured using the similar method and their results were summarized in Table S3. Corresponding RSD (0.09%–3.35%) and recovery (between 91.0% and 110.6%) were also obtained. Therefore, the fluorescent probe proposed is reliable and can be used for detecting VB12/tartrazine in real samples.

In summary, N,S-CQDs as a multifunctional sensor was prepared with excellent water solubility, salt tolerance and pH stability, and its preparation conditions were optimized. The probe possesses remarkable selectivity and high sensitivity for monitoring VB12/tartrazine. The Stern-Volmer plot indicated that the FL quenching of N,S-CQDs by VB12/tartrazine was mainly arisen from a static quenching. Moreover, the observed FL quenching was further analyzed by FRET theory. N,S-CQDs and VB12/tartrazine can form 1:1 complex and corresponding binding constant is 6.48 × 104/2.31 × 104 L mol−1. Significantly, the probe has been successfully utilized to assay VB12 in milk or vitamin drinks, and tartrazine in soda beverage or energy drinks with satisfactory recovery results. We expect that the N,S-CQDs will be widely applied in fluorescence related fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from Sichuan Provincial Science & Technology Fund for Applied Fundamental Research (2016JY0080), Doctor Start-up Fund (15E006) of China West Normal University, and Talent research fund of China West Normal University (17YC012).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.12.002.
